text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Human-Centered AI lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Human-centered AI keeps public health professionals, affected communities, and accountable decision-makers at the center of design, review, and action.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Human-centered AI keeps public health professionals, affected communities, and accountable decision-makers at the center of design, review, and action.
textPreview: Human-centered AI keeps public health professionals, affected communities, and accountable decision-makers at the center of design, revie...

text: Human-centered AI keeps public health purpose, staff expertise, and community accountability at the center. This is especially important when AI affects outreach, communication, prioritization, inspections, surveillance, or services.
textPreview: Human-centered AI keeps public health purpose, staff expertise, and community accountability at the center. This is especially important ...

text: AI should support judgment, not replace community relationships, public accountability, or final decisions in consequential workflows.
textPreview: AI should support judgment, not replace community relationships, public accountability, or final decisions in consequential workflows.

text:
Use this slide in the Human-Centered AI training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 6, 19, 41.
Emphasize the core guardrail: AI should support judgment, not replace community relationships, public accountability, or final decisions in consequential workflows.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Human-Centered AI in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Human-Centered AI training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 6, 19, 41.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Human-centered AI begins with the people affected by a workflow: public health staff, community members, partners, and decision-makers. The goal is not to automate as much as possible. The goal is to improve public health work while preserving human judgment, relationships, dignity, explainability, and accountability.
textPreview: Human-centered AI begins with the people affected by a workflow: public health staff, community members, partners, and decision-makers. T...

text: Definition: Human-centered AI is designed to assist people, not displace accountability. Public health staff, communities, and accountable leaders remain responsible for interpretation, judgment, relationship-building, and consequential.

Use this slide in the Human-Centered AI training module.
Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 6, 19, 41.
Reinforce this definition: Definition: Human-centered AI is designed to assist people, not displace accountability. Public health staff, communities, and accountable leaders remain responsible for interpretation, judgment, relationship-building, and consequential decisions.

Start with a public health problem and affected users, not with a tool. Define what humans must decide, approve, explain, or correct. Make AI outputs reviewable, editable, and contestable. Design for the staff who will use the system and the.
textPreview: Start with a public health problem and affected users, not with a tool. Define what humans must decide, approve, explain, or correct. Mak...

text: Name the decision owner for every AI-supported workflow. Require expert review before public-facing communication, enforcement, resource allocation, or clinical/care coordination action. Document correction, appeal, complaint, and escalation.
textPreview: Name the decision owner for every AI-supported workflow. Require expert review before public-facing communication, enforcement, resource ...

text: Use engagement to identify concerns before launch, especially for high-impact or community-facing systems. Explain AI use in plain language and invite feedback. Avoid deploying systems where affected people cannot understand or challenge the.

Start with Design Principles, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the Human-Centered AI training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 6, 19, 41.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Who is affected by the workflow and who has been consulted? What decision remains with a named human owner? How can people ask questions, correct information, or challenge an outcome?

text: For one proposed use case, write a human decision rule that defines what AI may support, what it may recommend, what a human must approve, and what the AI may not do.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Human decision rule, User and community needs summary, Appeal, correction, or feedback pathway.
